en.extract:
The cerulean warbler (Setophaga cerulea) is a small songbird in the family Parulidae. It is a long-distance migrant, breeding in eastern North American hardwood forests and wintering on the Eastern slope of the Andes in South America during non-breeding season, preferring subtropical forests.
It displays strong sexual dichromatism: Adult males have cerulean blue and white upperparts, with a black necklace across the breast and black streaks on the back and flanks. Females and immature birds have bluish-green upperparts, a pale stripe over the eye, no streaking, and are yellow below. All have two white wing bars and a thin, pointed bill.
The cerulean warbler is insectivorous and predominantly feeds on insect larvae, though it also comsumes winged insects. It forages for prey and nests high in forest canopies. Individuals are strongly territorial; males will defend areas of forests. Males arrive on breeding grounds about one to two weeks earlier than females. Breeding and incubation take place from late May to early June.
The species is rated as near threatened on the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN)'s Red List of endangered species, indicating it is under risk of becoming vulnerable in the near future. The population of the cerulean warbler has been under rapid decline, though in recent years this decline has slowed. Deforestation in its non-breeding range continues to reduce its available habitat.


Taxonomy and systematics
The cerulean warbler was described in 1810 as Sylvia cerulea by ornithologist Alexander Wilson. In 1811, Wilson described the female as a separate species, Sylvia rara. It was later realized that the two taxa were conspecific, and in 1838 the biologist Charles Lucien Bonaparte merged them together. In 1842, it was placed in the new genus Dendroica introduced by the ornithologist George Robert Gray. In 2011, Dendroica was merged into the genus Setophaga after molecular studies showed that the two genera were not well delineated. The change was implemented as part of the Fifty-second Supplement to the AOU Checklist of North American Birds. The genus name Setophaga is derived from the Greek ses, "moth",  and phagos, "eating". The specific name, cerulea, is derived from caeruleus, Latin for "blue", "azure".
Between 1897 and 1903, it was believed that the name Sylvia cærulea (an unjustified emendation of cerulea) had been preoccupied by the blue-gray gnatcatcher, Polioptila cærulea, as ornithologist John Latham had in 1793 moved the latter species to Sylvia. Therefore, the name Sylvia rara, an obsolete name for the female cerulean warbler, was accepted as Dendroica rara in the Eighth Supplement to the AOU Checklist of North American Birds. However, in 1903, it was found that the change was invalid, and the accepted name reverted to Dendroica cerulea.
The cerulean warbler's close relatives within the genus Setophaga include the Blackburnian warbler (S. fusca), blackpoll warbler (S. striata), chestnust-sided warbler, Wilson's warbler (S. pensylvanica) and bay-breasted warbler (S. castanea). Hybridization with the northern parula (S. americana) has been observed, indicating a close relationship.
The following cladograms show the position of the cerulean warbler among its closest relatives according to Lovette et al. 2010 (left), and Baiz et al. 2021 (right):


Description

The cerulean warbler is one of the smallest Setophaga wood-warblers, about 11 cm (4.3 in) long, weighing 8–10 g (0.28–0.35 oz), and having a wingspan of 20 cm (7.9 in). Males are slightly larger than females, and older males tend to also be larger. Wing chord measures 62–66 mm (2.4–2.6 in), while the tail is 40.6–42.7 mm (1.60–1.68 in) long. The culmen, or upper edge of the beak, measures 9.5–9.8 mm (0.37–0.39 in) long, 3.5 mm (0.14 in) wide and 3.5 mm (0.14 in) height.
The cerulean warbler is strongly sexually dichromatic. Adult males are a deep, cerulean blue over the entire back, and are white below. There are prominent dark streaks across the flanks. A "necklace"  or breast band, a line of color across the neck, varies from blue to near black. Older individuals have stronger colors across the entire body, including brighter whites, darker streaking, and wider breast bands. Adult females are yellow underneath, while the back and crown have a blue-green tint. Females also possess a distinct pale supercilium, or eye stripe, just above the eye.
Immature individuals are generally similar to adult females, though the back may be more yellowish. All fledged individuals, regardless of their age or sex, have two prominent white wing-bars and white spots on the tail.
The beak and legs of the cerulean warbler vary in color depending on an individual's age and sex. Adult males have a black beak, with the lower half a slightly lighter dark gray. Adult females have dark gray beaks that may become dark brown on the lower half. Recently fledged birds have a brownish beak. Adults have black legs and brown irises, while juveniles have dark brown legs and dark brown irises. Hatchlings have pink bare parts, including the beak and legs, and black eyes.
Their song is a buzzed, accelerating zray zray zray zray zeeee. The primary call is a buzzy, metallic zzee. Males use their songs to attract potential mates and to mark out their territory. Cerulean warblers are also capable of imitating the songs of other birds alongside their typical song. Females rarely sing, but often call when nesting. An alarm call is used to signal the arrival of a predator or another bird threatening the nest.


Distribution and habitat
The cerulean warbler's preferred summer habitat consists of large, unfragmented mature deciduous hardwood forests. Nesting takes place in the top canopy, often above heights of 30 m (98 ft), and territories are typically about 2 ha (4.9 acres). Foraging is done throughout the mid to high canopy. Generally, a forested area of greater than 700 ha (1,700 acres) is required to support a population of cerulean warblers. Riparian areas are commonly selected.
The breeding range extends from southern Tennessee to southern Ontario, and is bounded in the east by the Atlantic Ocean and on the west by the Great Plains. The majority of the population breeds in forests of the Appalachian mountains, with smaller numbers in forests throughout the United States and Canada. Historically, the cerulean warbler was common to abundant as far south as Alabama and Mississippi, but loss of habitat in these southern regions has caused the center of its breeding range to shift northwards.
The cerulean warbler migrates distances of over 5,000 km (3,100 mi) between its breeding and non-breeding ranges. In the spring, it takes an overland route through Central America, passing over water only to cross the Gulf of Mexico at the Yucatán Peninsula. In the autumn, it may overfly the Caribbean Sea, crossing from Florida to Cuba and onto continental South America. Migration takes from 40–70 days; much of this time is made up of stopovers, where birds will remain at one individual location for 2–18 days.
The non-breeding range covers a broad area of northern South America, and extends southwards along the Andes mountains. Individuals have been found as far south as Bolivia, while in the north it is often found throughout Colombia and Venezuela. The cerulean warbler prefers tall forest; its occurrence is therefore strongly correlated with the presence of old-growth subtropical forests or shade coffee plantations. It is found at altitudes of 500–2,000 m (1,600–6,600 ft) in its non-breeding range.
The cerulean warbler has been recorded as a vagrant to Iceland. One has also been recorded in Florianópolis in the far southeast of Brazil, over 2,500 km (1,600 mi) outside of its normal wintering range.


Behavior


Feeding

The cerulean warbler forages throughout the forest canopy, predominantly at middle to top layers. It hops from branch to branch, gleaning small soft-bodied insects from leaves and twigs. In the non-breeding season, it may also glean insects from flowers. Its preferred prey consists of butterfly and moth (lepidopteran larvae), though it supplements its diet with winged insects. In its non-breeding range, it preferentially forages on trees of the genus Inga.
Foraging is vertically segregated; males tend to forage at somewhat higher elevations in the canopy than females. The difference in height average is about 2 m (6.6 ft). They stay with mixed-species flocks as they forage, sharing spaces with species such as the American redstart (Setophaga ruticilla), Blackburnian warbler, and Tennessee warbler (Leiothlypis peregrina). The other species forage at different heights and substrates, preventing competition. At the high elevations and relatively sparser vegetation that the cerulean warbler prefers, the gleaning technique it uses is more effective than aerial maneuvring. Foraging locations tend to be lower than nest placements.


Breeding
The cerulean warbler is monogamous. Its short breeding season allows it to produce one brood a year. Individuals are very aggressive towards others of the same sex during the breeding season. Typical conflict comprises the use of song to mark a territory, as well as physical conflict such as attacks in which individuals may fall from heights of 20 m (66 ft). Female cerulean warblers have been observed to attack other females and destroy their eggs.
Nest construction takes about 7 days. The female lays from 3 to 5 eggs over the course of about 7 days, incubation lasts for around 12 days, and nestlings remain altricial for an average of 11 days. Only the female broods the nest, but the male will frequently provide food for the female and for the young.  The female occasionally responds with a call. During nesting, the female vocalizes a chip call whenever it leaves the nest. In response, the male returns to the nest, guarding it from a nearby perch. 

Extra-pair copulation, in which mating occurs outside of a monogamous pair, is known to occur. Based on blood analyses, several young may be sired by a different male than that which occupies the territory on which the young are found, making it likely that males travelling outside of their own territories seek extra-pair copulations. Males engage in mate guarding, in which they will remain close to the mate during periods of foraging, nest building, or nest site selection. The male will also frequently sing a "whisper song"; the female occasionally responds with a call.
During nest incubation, and when young are in the nestling stage, the female may also sing. The song, a more complex variation of the typical nest call, is a metallic zee zeet zee zeet zeet.
Both males and females are involved in nest construction and together choose a nest site. Sites are usually located on horizontal branches high in the forest canopy, and are usually shaded by vegetation. The cerulean warbler prefers to nest over an empty space where there are no branches for 1 m (3.3 ft) or more. The nest itself is made from tree bark, grasses, and lichens, woven together using caterpillar silk and spider thread. If a nesting attempt is unsuccessful, individuals will often reuse materials from an old nest, especially binding elements. Cerulean warblers have been observed to construct "double-decker" nests, in which a new nest is overlaid atop an existing one. Nearly all known nests have been in deciduous trees, but nesting has been recorded in pine trees in southern Indiana.
After the eggs have hatched, nestlings are fed by their parents, mainly consuming lepidopteran larvae. The fledglings become independent about 12 days after leaving the nest. Survival rates range from 34–62% for the fledgling stage of development. Newly fledged birds are often killed by chipmunks through predation. Young individuals have somewhat different feeding patterns than adults, preferring riparian areas with relatively little foliage.


Predators and parasites
The cerulean warbler's preferred nesting sites, located high in the forest canopy, reduce the prevalence of nest predation. However, its eggs and young still fall victim to predators such as raccoons, squirrels, chipmunks, snakes, and even other birds, in particular blue jays (Cyanocitta cristata).
The cerulean warbler is known to be parasitized by blood parasites such as Haemoproteus paruli. Nest parasitism by brown-headed cowbirds (Molothrus ater) occurs, but is less common in the dense interior canopy the cerulean warbler prefers.


Status and conservation

The cerulean warbler is currently rated a near threatened species by the International Union for Conservation of Nature. It is the fastest declining Neotropical migrant songbird. Among the many threats it faces, its wintering habitat in the northern Andes is dwindling rapidly. Cerulean warblers depend on shade coffee plantations for the mature forest habitat they require. This traditional farming technique faces pressure from fluctuating world coffee prices, and many tracts of shade coffee are being converted to higher-yield sun coffee or other crops.
In fragmented forest areas, the cerulean warbler is vulnerable to nest parasitism by the brown-headed cowbird. Historically, the preferred habitat of tall forest interiors protected it from brood parasitism, but deforestation has caused the cerulean warbler to often choose nest locations accessible to cowbirds.
This bird's numbers are declining faster than any other warbler species in the USA; its population in 2006 was less than one-fifth of what it was 40 years before. The American Bird Conservancy (ABC) is working with its Colombian partner, Fundación ProAves, to protect wintering habitat for cerulean warblers and other migratory songbirds. In 2008, the partnership created the Cerulean Warbler Bird Reserve, the first protected area created for a neotropical migrant. In an effort to advance protection of the cerulean warbler, ABC and its South American partners (Fundacion ProAves, ECOAN and Fundacion Jocotoco), in 2009 produced a Cerulean Warbler Wintering Ground Conservation Plan.


In art and culture

Artist and ornithologist John James Audubon illustrates the cerulean warbler in Birds of America (London, 1827–38) as Plate 48, where two birds are shown perched in a dahoon holly bush. The painting was originally produced in 1822, and engraved and colored by Robert Havell's London workshops. The original watercolor by Audubon was purchased by the New-York Historical Society, where it remains as of 2022.
The novelist Jonathan Franzen uses the cerulean warbler as a plot device in his 2010 novel Freedom.
In San Vicente, Colombia, the cerulean warbler is commemorated in an annual migratory bird festival.
The Man in the Yellow Hat publishes a book about the cerulean warbler in the episode "The Big Picture" of Curious George.


References


External links

"Cerulean warbler media". Internet Bird Collection.
Cerulean warbler photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)

The population of the cerulean warbler has been under rapid decline, though in recent years this decline has slowed. However, in 1903, it was found that the change was invalid, and the accepted name reverted to ''Dendroica cerulea''.{{Cite journal |last=Allen |first=J. (2010){{Cite journal |last1=Lovette |first1=Irby J. |last2=Pérez-Emán |first2=Jorge L. |last3=Sullivan |first3=John P. |last4=Banks |first4=Richard C. |last5=Fiorentino |first5=Isabella |last6=Córdoba-Córdoba |first6=Sergio |last7=Echeverry-Galvis |first7=María |last8=Barker |first8=F. Keith |last9=Burns |first9=Kevin J. |last10=Klicka |first10=John |last11=Lanyon |first11=Scott M. |date=2010 |title=A comprehensive multilocus phylogeny for the wood-warblers and a revised classification of the Parulidae (Aves) |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1055790310003179 |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=57 |issue=2 |pages=753–770 |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2010.07.018 |pmid=20696258 |bibcode=2010MolPE..57..753L |issn=1055-7903|url-access=subscription }} {{Clade |1={{Clade |1={{Clade |1=''[[Setophaga caerulescens]]'' (Black-throated blue warbler) |2=''[[Setophaga magnolia]]'' (Magnolia warbler) }} |2={{Clade |1='''''[[Setophaga cerulea]]''''' ('''Cerulean warbler''') |2={{Clade |1=''[[Setophaga americana]]'' (Northern parula) |2=''[[Setophaga pitiayumi]]'' (Tropical parula) }} }} |3={{Clade |1={{Clade |1=''[[Setophaga castanea]]'' (Bay-breasted warbler) |2=''[[Setophaga fusca]]'' (Blackburnian warbler) }} |2={{Clade |1=''[[Setophaga petechia]]'' (Yellow warbler) |2={{Clade |1=''[[Setophaga pensylvanica]]'' (Chestnut-sided warbler) |2=''[[Setophaga striata]]'' (Blackpoll warbler) }} }} }} }} }} {{Col-float-break}} Baiz et al. (2021) {{Clade |1={{Clade |1={{Clade |1=''[[Setophaga magnolia]]'' (Magnolia warbler) |2={{Clade |1=''[[Setophaga americana]]'' (Northern parula) |2=''[[Setophaga pitiayumi]]'' (Tropica parula) }} }} |2={{Clade |1='''''[[Setophaga cerulea]]''''' ('''Cerulean warbler''') |2={{Clade |1=''[[Setophaga fusca]]'' (Blackburnian warbler) |2={{Clade |1={{Clade |1=''[[Setophaga castanea]]'' (Bay-breasted warbler) |2=''[[Setophaga striata]]'' (Blackpoll warbler) }} |2={{Clade |1=''[[Setophaga pensylvanica]]'' (Chestnut-sided warbler) |2=''[[Setophaga petechia]]'' (Yellow warbler)) }} }} }} }} }} }} {{Col-float-end}} ==Description== [[File:Cerulean Warbler close-up.jpg|alt=The head and upper body of a male Cerulean Warbler are shown in this photo by the US Fish and Wildlife Service.|left|thumb|Adult male Cerulean warbler close-up, showing "necklace" and streaking]] [[File:Cerulean Warbler Song.wav|left|thumb|Song and calls]] The cerulean warbler is one of the smallest ''Setophaga'' wood-warblers, about {{Convert|11|cm|in|abbr=on}} long, weighing {{convert|8|-|10|g|abbr=on|sp=us|oz}}, and having a [[wingspan]] of {{Convert|20|cm|in|abbr=on}}. Males are slightly larger than females, and older males tend to also be larger.{{Cite journal |title=Plumages, Molts, and Morphology - Cerulean Warbler - Setophaga cerulea - Birds of the World |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/cerwar/cur/appearance#meas |access-date=2022-05-15 |website=birdsoftheworld.org |date=2020 |doi=10.2173/bow.cerwar.01 |s2cid=216248275 |language=en |last1=Buehler |first1=David A. |last2=Hamel |first2=Paul B. |last3=Boves |first3=Than |url-access=subscription }} [[Wing chord (biology)|Wing chord]] measures {{convert|62|-|66|mm|abbr=on|sp=us|in}}, while the tail is {{convert|40.6|-|42.7|mm|abbr=on|sp=us|in}} long. The culmen, or upper edge of the [[beak]], measures {{convert|9.5|-|9.8|mm|abbr=on|sp=us|in}} long, {{Convert|3.5|mm|in|abbr=on}} wide and {{Convert|3.5|mm|in|abbr=on}} height. The cerulean warbler is strongly sexually dichromatic. Adult males are a deep, cerulean blue over the entire back, and are white below. There are prominent dark streaks across the flanks. A "necklace" or breast band, a line of color across the neck, varies from blue to near black. Older individuals have stronger colors across the entire body, including brighter whites, darker streaking, and wider breast bands.{{Cite journal |last1=Boves |first1=Than J. |last2=Buehler |first2=David A. |last3=Wood |first3=Petra B. |last4=Rodewald |first4=Amanda D. |last5=Larkin |first5=Jeffrey L. |last6=Keyser |first6=Patrick D. |last7=Wigley |first7=T. Ben |date=2014-01-01 |title=Multiple plumage traits convey information about age and within-age-class qualities of a canopy-dwelling songbird, the Cerulean Warbler |journal=The Auk |volume=131 |issue=1 |pages=20–31 |doi=10.1642/AUK-13-191.1 |s2cid=54716104 |issn=1938-4254|doi-access=free |bibcode=2014Auk...131...20B }} Adult females are yellow underneath, while the back and {{birdgloss|crown}} have a blue-green tint.Environment and Climate Change Canada. 2020. Recovery Strategy for the Cerulean 5 Warbler (Setophaga cerulea) in Canada [Proposed]. Species at Risk Act Recovery 6 Strategy Series. Environment and Climate Change Canada, Ottawa. vii + 54 pp. Females also possess a distinct pale [[supercilium]], or eye stripe, just above the eye.{{Cite web |last=Canada |first=Environment and Climate Change |date=2011-10-26 |title=Cerulean warbler (Dendroica cerulea): COSEWIC assessment and status report 2010 |url=https://www.canada.ca/en/environment-climate-change/services/species-risk-public-registry/cosewic-assessments-status-reports/cerulean-warbler-2010.html |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=www.canada.ca}} Immature individuals are generally similar to adult females, though the back may be more yellowish. All [[fledged]] individuals, regardless of their age or sex, have two prominent white wing-bars and white spots on the tail.{{cite book |last1=Curson |first1=Jon |last2=Quinn |first2=David |last3=Beadle |first3=David |title=New World Warblers |location=London |year=1994 |publisher=Christopher Helm |isbn=0-7136-3932-6 |pages=152–153}} The beak and [[Bird legs|legs]] of the cerulean warbler vary in color depending on an individual's age and sex. Adult males have a black beak, with the lower half a slightly lighter dark gray. Adult females have dark gray beaks that may become dark brown on the lower half. Recently fledged birds have a brownish beak. Adults have black legs and brown irises, while juveniles have dark brown legs and dark brown irises. [[Hatchling|Hatchlings]] have pink bare parts, including the beak and legs, and black eyes. Their [[Bird vocalization|song]] is a buzzed, accelerating ''zray zray zray zray zeeee''. The primary call is a buzzy, metallic ''zzee''. Males use their songs to attract potential mates and to mark out their territory. Cerulean warblers are also capable of imitating the songs of other birds alongside their typical song.{{Cite journal |last1=Boves |first1=Than J. |last2=Buehler |first2=David A. |last3=Massey |first3=Phillip C. |date=2010-09-01 |title=Interspecific Song Imitation by a Cerulean Warbler |url=https://bioone.org/journals/the-wilson-journal-of-ornithology/volume-122/issue-3/09-157.1/Interspecific-Song-Imitation-by-a-Cerulean-Warbler/10.1676/09-157.1.full |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=122 |issue=3 |pages=583–587 |doi=10.1676/09-157.1 |s2cid=86728447 |issn=1559-4491|url-access=subscription }} Females rarely sing, but often call when nesting. An alarm call is used to signal the arrival of a predator or another bird threatening the nest.{{Cite journal |last1=McKillip |first1=Sarah Register |last2=Islam |first2=Kamal |date=2009 |title=Vocalization Attributes of Cerulean Warbler Song and Pairing Status |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=121 |issue=2 |pages=273–282 |doi=10.1676/08-094.1 |jstor=20616897 |s2cid=85243172 |issn=1559-4491}} ==Distribution and habitat== The cerulean warbler's preferred summer habitat consists of large, unfragmented mature [[deciduous]] hardwood forests.{{Cite news |date=2014-11-13 |title=Cerulean Warbler |url=https://www.audubon.org/field-guide/bird/cerulean-warbler |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=Audubon |language=en}} Nesting takes place in the top [[Canopy (biology)|canopy]], often above heights of {{Convert|30|m|ft|abbr=on}}, and territories are typically about {{Convert|2|ha|acre|abbr=on}}. [[Foraging]] is done throughout the mid to high canopy. Generally, a forested area of greater than {{Convert|700|ha|acre|abbr=on}} is required to support a population of cerulean warblers.{{Cite journal |last1=Perkins |first1=Kelly A. |last2=Wood |first2=Petra Bohall |date=1 June 2014 |title=Selection of forest canopy gaps by male Cerulean Warblers in West Virginia |url=https://bioone.org/journals/the-wilson-journal-of-ornithology/volume-126/issue-2/13-067.1/Selection-of-forest-canopy-gaps-by-male-Cerulean-Warblers-in/10.1676/13-067.1.full |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=126 |issue=2 |pages=288–297 |doi=10.1676/13-067.1 |bibcode=2014TWJO..126..288P |s2cid=86788132 |issn=1559-4491|url-access=subscription }} [[Riparian zone|Riparian]] areas are commonly selected.{{Cite journal |last1=Wessels |first1=Jacob L. |last2=Boves |first2=Than J. |date=6 February 2021 |title=Cerulean Warblers in the Ozark region: habitat selection, breeding biology, survival, and space use |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jofo.12358 |journal=Journal of Field Ornithology |language=en |volume=92 |issue=1 |pages=54–66 |doi=10.1111/jofo.12358 |s2cid=233624909 |issn=0273-8570 |via=Wiley Online Library|url-access=subscription }} The [[Species distribution|breeding range]] extends from southern [[Tennessee]] to southern [[Ontario]], and is bounded in the east by the [[Atlantic Ocean]] and on the west by the [[Great Plains]]. The majority of the population breeds in forests of the [[Appalachian Mountains|Appalachian mountains]],{{Cite web |title=Cerulean Warbler |url=https://abcbirds.org/bird/cerulean-warbler/ |access-date=2022-05-16 |website=American Bird Conservancy |language=en}} with smaller numbers in forests throughout the [[United States]] and [[Canada]]. Historically, the cerulean warbler was common to abundant as far south as [[Alabama]] and [[Mississippi]], but loss of habitat in these southern regions has caused the center of its breeding range to shift northwards.{{Cite journal |last1=Hamel |first1=Paul B. |last2=Dawson |first2=Deanna K. |last3=Keyser |first3=Patrick D. |date=2004 |title=How We Can Learn More about the Cerulean Warbler (Dendroica cerulea) |journal=The Auk |volume=121 |issue=1 |pages=7–14 |doi=10.2307/4090050 |jstor=4090050 |issn=0004-8038}} The cerulean warbler migrates distances of over {{Convert|5000|km|mi|abbr=on}} between its breeding and non-breeding ranges. In the spring, it takes an overland route through [[Central America]], passing over water only to cross the [[Gulf of Mexico]] at the [[Yucatán Peninsula]]. In the autumn, it may overfly the [[Caribbean Sea]], crossing from [[Florida]] to [[Cuba]] and onto continental [[South America]]. Migration takes from 40–70 days; much of this time is made up of stopovers, where birds will remain at one individual location for 2–18 days.{{Cite journal |last1=Delancey |first1=Clayton D. |last2=Islam |first2=Kamal |last3=Kramer |first3=Gunnar R. |last4=MacDonald |first4=Garrett J. |last5=Sharp |first5=Alexander R. |last6=Connare |first6=Brandon M. |date=2020-01-01 |title=Geolocators reveal migration routes, stopover sites, and nonbreeding dispersion in a population of Cerulean Warblers |journal=Animal Migration |language=en |volume=7 |issue=1 |pages=19–26 |doi=10.1515/ami-2020-0003 |s2cid=214765177 |issn=2084-8838|doi-access=free }} The non-breeding range covers a broad area of northern South America, and extends southwards along the [[Andes|Andes mountains]]. Individuals have been found as far south as [[Bolivia]], while in the north it is often found throughout [[Colombia]] and [[Venezuela]]. The cerulean warbler prefers tall forest; its occurrence is therefore strongly correlated with the presence of old-growth subtropical forests or [[Shade-grown coffee|shade coffee]] [[plantation]]s.{{Cite journal |last1=Colorado |first1=Gabriel J. |last2=Hamel |first2=Paul B. |last3=Rodewald |first3=Amanda D. |last4=Mehlman |first4=David |date=2012 |title=Advancing our understanding of the non-breeding distribution of Cerulean Warbler (Setophaga cerulea) in the Andes |url=https://research.fs.usda.gov/treesearch/45155 |journal=Ornitologia Neotropical 23:307–315 |language=en |volume=23 |pages=307–315}} It is found at altitudes of {{convert|500|-|2000|m|abbr=on|sp=us|feet}} in its non-breeding range.{{Cite journal |last1=Muñoz |first1=Jenny M. |last2=Colorado |first2=Gabriel J. |date=2012 |title=FORAGING ECOLOGY OF THE CERULEAN WARBLER (SETOPHAGA CERULEA) IN ANDEAN AGROFORESTRY ECOSYSTEMS. |url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/133599 |journal=Ornitologia Neotropical |volume=23 |pages=355–362 |via=Searchable Ornithology Research Archive}} The cerulean warbler has been recorded as a [[Vagrancy (biology)|vagrant]] to [[Iceland]].Þráinsson, Gunnlaugur (1997) Palm Warbler and Cerulean Warbler in Iceland – new to the Western Palearctic ''[[Birding World]]'' 10(10): 392–393 One has also been recorded in [[Florianópolis]] in the far southeast of Brazil, over {{cvt|2,500|km}} outside of its normal wintering range.{{Cite journal |last1=de Farias |first1=Fernando Bittencourt |last2=Dalpaz |first2=Larissa |date=2019-06-01 |title=First documented record of Cerulean Warbler Setophaga cerulea (Parulidae) in Brazil |journal=Revista Brasileira de Ornitologia |language=en |volume=27 |issue=2 |pages=132–134 |doi=10.1007/BF03544459 |s2cid=213225016 |issn=2178-7875|doi-access=free }} ==Behavior== === Feeding === [[File:Setophaga cerulea Weldon Spring 2.jpg|thumb|Female cerulean warbler in the nest]] The cerulean warbler forages throughout the forest canopy, predominantly at middle to top layers. It hops from branch to branch, [[Gleaning (birds)|gleaning]] small soft-bodied insects from leaves and twigs.{{Cite journal |last1=Newell |first1=Felicity L. |last2=Beachy |first2=Tiffany-Ahren |last3=Rodewald |first3=Amanda D. |last4=Rengifo |first4=Carlos G. |last5=Ausprey |first5=Ian J. |last6=Rodewald |first6=Paul G. |date=2014 |title=Foraging behavior of Cerulean Warblers during the breeding and non-breeding seasons: evidence for the breeding currency hypothesis |journal=Journal of Field Ornithology |volume=85 |issue=3 |pages=310–320 |doi=10.1111/jofo.12070 |jstor=24617812 |issn=0273-8570}} In the non-breeding season, it may also glean insects from flowers. Its preferred prey consists of butterfly and moth ([[lepidoptera]]n larvae), though it supplements its diet with winged insects. In its non-breeding range, it preferentially forages on trees of the genus ''[[Inga]]''.{{Cite journal |last1=Newell |first1=Felicity L. |last2=Beachy |first2=Tiffany-Ahren |last3=Rodewald |first3=Amanda D. |last4=Rengifo |first4=Carlos G. |last5=Ausprey |first5=Ian J. |last6=Rodewald |first6=Paul G. |date=26 May 2014 |title=Foraging behavior of migrant warblers in mixed-species flocks in Venezuelan shade coffee: interspecific differences, tree species selection, and effects of drought: Foraging Behavior of Warblers in Shade Coffee |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jofo.12056 |journal=Journal of Field Ornithology |language=en |volume=85 |issue=2 |pages=134–151 |doi=10.1111/jofo.12056|url-access=subscription }} Foraging is vertically segregated; males tend to forage at somewhat higher elevations in the canopy than females. The difference in height average is about {{Convert|2|m|ft|abbr=on}}. They stay with [[Mixed-species foraging flock|mixed-species flocks]] as they forage, sharing spaces with species such as the [[American redstart]] (''Setophaga ruticilla''), Blackburnian warbler, and [[Tennessee warbler]] (''Leiothlypis peregrina''). The other species forage at different heights and substrates, preventing competition.{{Cite journal |last1=Bakermans |first1=Marja H. |last2=Rodewald |first2=Amanda D. |last3=Vitz |first3=Andrew C. |last4=Rengifo |first4=Carlos |date=11 April 2011 |title=Migratory bird use of shade coffee: the role of structural and floristic features |journal=Agroforestry Systems |language=en |volume=85 |issue=1 |pages=85–94 |doi=10.1007/s10457-011-9389-0 |s2cid=18036286 |issn=1572-9680}} At the high elevations and relatively sparser vegetation that the cerulean warbler prefers, the gleaning technique it uses is more effective than aerial maneuvring.{{Cite journal |last1=Bakermans |first1=Marja H. |last2=Vitz |first2=Andrew C. |last3=Rodewald |first3=Amanda D. |last4=Rengifo |first4=Carlos G. |date=2009-11-01 |title=Migratory songbird use of shade coffee in the Venezuelan Andes with implications for conservation of cerulean warbler |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0006320709002389 |journal=Biological Conservation |language=en |volume=142 |issue=11 |pages=2476–2483 |doi=10.1016/j.biocon.2009.05.018 |bibcode=2009BCons.142.2476B |issn=0006-3207|url-access=subscription }} Foraging locations tend to be lower than nest placements. === Breeding === The cerulean warbler is [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]].{{Cite web |title=Setophaga cerulea (Cerulean warbler) - Michigan Natural Features Inventory |url=https://mnfi.anr.msu.edu/species/description/11182/Setophaga-cerulea |access-date=2022-05-16 |website=mnfi.anr.msu.edu}} Its short breeding season allows it to produce one brood a year. Individuals are very aggressive towards others of the same sex during the breeding season. Typical conflict comprises the use of song to mark a territory, as well as physical conflict such as attacks in which individuals may fall from heights of {{Convert|20|m|ft|abbr=on}}.{{Cite journal |last=Hamel |first=Paul B. |date=2000 |title=Cerulean warbler (Dendroica cerulea) |url=https://research.fs.usda.gov/treesearch/1820 |journal=In: Poole, A.; Gill, F., Eds. The Birds of North America—life Histories for the 21st Century; No. 511. Philadelphia, PA: The Birds of North America, Inc.: 1-20. |language=en}} Female cerulean warblers have been observed to attack other females and destroy their eggs.{{Cite journal |last1=Boves |first1=Than J. |last2=Buehler |first2=David A. |last3=Boves |first3=N. Emily |date=1 June 2011 |title=Conspecific Egg Destruction by a Female Cerulean Warbler |url=https://bioone.org/journals/the-wilson-journal-of-ornithology/volume-123/issue-2/10-151.1/Conspecific-Egg-Destruction-by-a-Female-Cerulean-Warbler/10.1676/10-151.1.full |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=123 |issue=2 |pages=401–403 |doi=10.1676/10-151.1 |s2cid=86085730 |issn=1559-4491|url-access=subscription }} [[Nest]] construction takes about 7 days. The female lays from 3 to 5 eggs over the course of about 7 days, [[Egg incubation|incubation]] lasts for around 12 days, and nestlings remain [[Altricial#Altriciality|altricial]] for an average of 11 days. Only the female broods the nest, but the male will frequently provide food for the female and for the young.{{Cite journal |last1=Barg |first1=Jennifer J. |last2=Jones |first2=Jason |last3=Girvan |first3=M. Katharine |last4=Robertson |first4=Raleigh J. |date=2006 |title=Within-Pair Interactions and Parental Behavior of Cerulean Warblers Breeding in Eastern Ontario |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=118 |issue=3 |pages=316–325 |doi=10.1676/05-027.1 |jstor=20455884 |s2cid=18017054 |issn=1559-4491}} The female occasionally responds with a call. During nesting, the female vocalizes a ''chip'' call whenever it leaves the nest. In response, the male returns to the nest, guarding it from a nearby perch. [[File:Figs 55 56 Cerulean Warbler eggs The warblers of North America 1914.png|left|thumb|Cerulean warbler eggs]] [[Extra-pair copulation]], in which mating occurs outside of a monogamous pair, is known to occur.{{Cite journal |last1=Delancey |first1=Clayton D. |last2=Islam |first2=Kamal |date=2020-11-03 |title=Analysis of microhabitat characteristics at roost sites of Cerulean Warblers |journal=PLOS ONE |language=en |volume=15 |issue=11 |article-number=e0241501 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0241501 |issn=1932-6203 |pmc=7608899 |pmid=33141873 |bibcode=2020PLoSO..1541501D |doi-access=free}} Based on blood analyses, several young may be sired by a different male than that which occupies the territory on which the young are found, making it likely that males travelling outside of their own territories seek extra-pair copulations. Males engage in mate guarding, in which they will remain close to the mate during periods of foraging, nest building, or nest site selection. The male will also frequently sing a "whisper song"; the female occasionally responds with a call. During nest incubation, and when young are in the nestling stage, the female may also sing.{{Cite journal |last1=MacDonald |first1=Garrett J. |last2=Delancey |first2=Clayton D. |last3=Islam |first3=Kamal |date=20 June 2019 |title=Novel vocalizations, including song, from 2 female Cerulean Warblers (Setophaga cerulea) |url=https://bioone.org/journals/the-wilson-journal-of-ornithology/volume-131/issue-2/18-104/Novel-vocalizations-including-song-from-2-female-Cerulean-Warblers-Setophaga/10.1676/18-104.full |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=131 |issue=2 |pages=366–373 |doi=10.1676/18-104 |s2cid=145866306 |issn=1559-4491|url-access=subscription }} The song, a more complex variation of the typical nest call, is a metallic ''zee zeet zee zeet zeet''. Both males and females are involved in nest construction and together choose a nest site. Sites are usually located on horizontal branches high in the forest canopy, and are usually shaded by vegetation. The cerulean warbler prefers to nest over an empty space where there are no branches for {{Convert|1|m|ft|abbr=on}} or more. The nest itself is made from tree bark, grasses, and lichens, woven together using caterpillar silk and spider thread.{{Cite web |last1=Shen |first1=Mailing Address |last2=Luray |first2=oah National Park 3655 U. S. Highway 211 East |last3=Us |first3=VA 22835 Phone: 540 999-3500 Emergency Phone: 1-800-732-0911 Contact |title=Cerulean Warbler - Shenandoah National Park (U.S. National Park Service) |url=https://www.nps.gov/shen/learn/nature/cerulean_warbler.htm |access-date=2022-05-18 |website=www.nps.gov |language=en}} If a nesting attempt is unsuccessful, individuals will often reuse materials from an old nest, especially binding elements. Cerulean warblers have been observed to construct "double-decker" nests, in which a new nest is overlaid atop an existing one.{{Cite journal |last1=Nemes |first1=Claire E. |last2=Islam |first2=Kamal |last3=Pirtle |first3=Daniel M. |date=2015 |title=First Documentation of a "Double-Decker" Cerulean Warbler (Setophaga cerulea) Nest |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=127 |issue=3 |pages=534–538 |doi=10.1676/14-157.1 |jstor=24640624 |s2cid=86084500 |issn=1559-4491}} Nearly all known nests have been in deciduous trees, but nesting has been recorded in pine trees in southern Indiana.{{cite journal |last1=Sharp |first1=Alexander |last2=Connare |first2=Brandon |last3=Islam |first3=Kamal |date=2021 |title=Novel nest tree selection by a deciduous forest New World warbler |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=133 |issue=4 |pages=640–645 |doi=10.1676/20-0016 }} After the eggs have hatched, nestlings are fed by their parents, mainly consuming lepidopteran larvae.{{Cite web |title=Cerulean warbler |url=http://www.ontario.ca/page/cerulean-warbler |access-date=2022-05-18 |website=ontario.ca |date=18 July 2014 |language=en}} The fledglings become independent about 12 days after leaving the nest. Survival rates range from 34–62% for the fledgling stage of development. Newly fledged birds are often killed by [[chipmunk]]s through predation. Young individuals have somewhat different feeding patterns than adults, preferring riparian areas with relatively little foliage.{{cite journal | doi=10.1093/condor/duz063 | title=Radio-tracking reveals insight into survival and dynamic habitat selection of fledgling Cerulean Warblers | year=2020 | last1=Boves | first1=Than J. | last2=Stoleson | first2=Scott H. | last3=Larkin | first3=Jeffery L. | last4=Raybuck | first4=Douglas W. | journal=The Condor | volume=122 | issue=1 | article-number=duz063 | doi-access=free }} === Predators and parasites === The cerulean warbler's preferred nesting sites, located high in the forest canopy, reduce the prevalence of nest predation. However, its eggs and young still fall victim to predators such as [[raccoon]]s, [[squirrel]]s, [[chipmunk]]s, [[snake]]s, and even other birds, in particular [[blue jay]]s (''Cyanocitta cristata'').{{Cite web |title=Cerulean Warbler |url=https://www.dept.psu.edu/nkbiology/naturetrail/speciespages/cerulean_warbler.html |access-date=2022-06-04 |website=www.dept.psu.edu}}{{Cite web |title=Cerulean Warbler Guide - New York Natural Heritage Program |url=https://guides.nynhp.org/cerulean-warbler/ |access-date=2022-06-04 |website=guides.nynhp.org}} The cerulean warbler is known to be [[Parasitism|parasitized]] by blood parasites such as ''[[Haemoproteus|Haemoproteus paruli]].''{{Cite journal |last1=Gibb |first1=Christine E. |last2=Jones |first2=Jason |last3=Girvan |first3=M. Kate |last4=Barg |first4=Jennifer J. |last5=Robertson |first5=Raleigh J. |date=2011-02-15 |title=Geographic variation in prevalence and parasitemia of Haemoproteus paruli in the cerulean warbler (Dendroica cerulea) |url=https://cdnsciencepub.com/doi/abs/10.1139/z05-043 |journal=Canadian Journal of Zoology |volume=83 |issue=4 |pages=626–629 |language=en |doi=10.1139/z05-043|url-access=subscription }} [[Brood parasite|Nest parasitism]] by [[brown-headed cowbird]]s (''Molothrus ater'') occurs, but is less common in the dense interior canopy the cerulean warbler prefers.{{Cite web |last=Canada |first=Environment and Climate Change |date=2011-03-16 |title=Cerulean warbler (Dendroica cerulea): management plan 2011 |url=https://www.canada.ca/en/environment-climate-change/services/species-risk-public-registry/management-plans/cerulean-warbler-2011.html |access-date=2022-05-16 |website=www.canada.ca}} ==Status and conservation== [[File:"Azure Warbler" in Audubon's Birds of America.jpg|alt="Azure Warbler" in Birds of America|thumb|"Azure Warbler" in ''Birds of America''|200x200px]] The cerulean warbler is currently rated a [[near threatened]] species by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]]. It is the fastest declining [[Neotropical realm|Neotropical]] migrant songbird. Among the many threats it faces, its wintering habitat in the northern Andes is dwindling rapidly. Cerulean warblers depend on shade coffee plantations for the mature forest habitat they require. This traditional farming technique faces pressure from fluctuating world coffee prices, and many tracts of shade coffee are being converted to higher-yield sun coffee or other crops.{{Cite journal |last1=González |first1=Ana M |last2=Wilson |first2=Scott |last3=Bayly |first3=Nicholas J |last4=Hobson |first4=Keith A |date=5 May 2020 |title=Contrasting the suitability of shade coffee agriculture and native forest as overwinter habitat for Canada Warbler (Cardellina canadensis) in the Colombian Andes |url=https://academic.oup.com/condor/article/122/2/duaa011/5816297 |journal=The Condor |volume=122 |issue=2 |article-number=duaa011 |doi=10.1093/condor/duaa011 |via=Oxford University Press|doi-access=free }} In fragmented forest areas, the cerulean warbler is vulnerable to nest parasitism by the brown-headed cowbird. Historically, the preferred habitat of tall forest interiors protected it from brood parasitism, but deforestation has caused the cerulean warbler to often choose nest locations accessible to cowbirds.{{Cite journal |last1=Oliarnyk |first1=Catherine J. |last2=Robertson |first2=R. J. |date=1996 |title=Breeding behavior and reproductive success of Cerulean Warblers in southeastern Ontario |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v108n04/p0673-p0684.pdf |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |language=en |volume=108 |pages=673–684 |via=Searchable Ornithology Research Archive}} This bird's numbers are declining faster than any other warbler species in the USA; its population in 2006 was less than one-fifth of what it was 40 years before.{{Cite web |date=2006-12-07 |title=Federal Agency Won't List Disappearing Eastern Songbird As 'threatened' |url=https://www.audubon.org/news/federal-agency-wont-list-disappearing-eastern-songbird-threatened |access-date=2022-05-18 |website=Audubon |language=en}} The [[American Bird Conservancy]] (ABC) is working with its Colombian partner, [[Fundación ProAves]], to protect wintering habitat for cerulean warblers and other migratory songbirds. In 2008, the partnership created the [[Cerulean Warbler Bird Reserve]], the first protected area created for a neotropical migrant.{{cite magazine |last=Parr |first=Michael J. |date=2007 |title=Saving the Cerulean Warbler |url=https://www.aba.org/birding_archive_files/v39n5p24.pdf |magazine=Birding |publisher=American Birdwatching Association |access-date=2022-06-03}} In an effort to advance protection of the cerulean warbler, ABC and its South American partners (Fundacion ProAves, ECOAN and Fundacion Jocotoco), in 2009 produced a Cerulean Warbler Wintering Ground Conservation Plan.{{Cite web |date=2008-01-11 |title=Developing a Cerulean Warbler wintering Conservation Plan |url=https://proaves.org/en/developing-a-cerulean-warbler-wintering-conservation-plan/ |access-date=2022-05-22 |website=Fundación ProAves - por las aves y su hábitat en Colombia |language=en-US |author1=Dogie_To }} ==In art and culture== [[File:San Vicente Migratory Bird Festival (4604552424).jpg|left|thumb|San Vicente Migratory Bird Festival |140x140px]] Artist and ornithologist [[John James Audubon]] illustrates the cerulean warbler in ''[[Birds of America (book)|Birds of America]]'' (London, 1827–38) as Plate 48, where two birds are shown perched in a [[Ilex cassine|dahoon holly]] bush. The painting was originally produced in 1822, and engraved and colored by [[Havell family|Robert Havell]]'s London workshops. The original watercolor by Audubon was purchased by the [[New-York Historical Society]], where it remains as of 2022.{{Cite web |title=Cerulean Warbler (Setophaga cerulea), Study for Havell pl. 48 |url=https://emuseum.nyhistory.org/objects/27395/cerulean-warbler-setophaga-cerulea-study-for-havell-pl-4 |access-date=2022-05-16 |website=New-York Historical Society}} The novelist [[Jonathan Franzen]] uses the cerulean warbler as a plot device in his 2010 novel ''[[Freedom (Franzen novel)|Freedom]]''.{{Cite book |last=Franzen |first=Jonathan |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=cWQJJIgJNtUC |title=Freedom |date=2010-08-31 |publisher=Farrar, Straus and Giroux |isbn=978-1-4299-7943-6 |language=en}} In [[San Vicente de Chucurí|San Vicente]], Colombia, the cerulean warbler is commemorated in an annual [[Bird migration|migratory bird]] festival.{{Cite web |date=2021-10-15 |title=ProAves Celebrates the arrival of the Extreme Travelers |url=https://proaves.org/en/proaves-celebrates-the-arrival-of-the-extreme-travelers/ |access-date=2022-05-18 |website=Fundación ProAves - por las aves y su hábitat en Colombia |language=en-US |last1=Salaman |first1=Paul }} The Man in the Yellow Hat publishes a book about the cerulean warbler in the episode "The Big Picture" of ''[[Curious George (TV series)|Curious George]]''.{{Cite web |title=PBS KIDS Goes Green All Week on WXXI-TV |url=https://www.wxxi.org/highlights/2011/08/pbs-kids-goes-green-all-week-wxxi-tv |access-date=2022-05-29 |website=WXXI |language=en}}{{Cite journal |last1=Hamel |first1=Paul B. |last2=Mehlman |first2=David |last3=Herzog |first3=Sebastian |last4=Rosenberg |first4=Kenneth C. |last5=Jones |first5=Jason |date=2012 |title=Passing the baton of action from research to conservation implementation for Cerulean Warbler (Setophaga cerulea) |url=https://research.fs.usda.gov/treesearch/46293 |journal=Ornitologia Neotropical. 23: 367–378 |language=en |volume=23 |pages=367–375}} {{-}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Setophaga cerulea|Cerulean warbler}} {{Wikispecies|Setophaga cerulea}} *{{InternetBirdCollection|cerulean-warbler-dendroica-cerulea|Cerulean warbler}} *{{VIREO|Cerulean+Warbler|Cerulean warbler}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q25066647}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Setophaga|cerulean warbler]] [[Category:Birds of Appalachia (United States)]] [[Category:Native birds of the Eastern United States]] [[Category:Native birds of the Northeastern United States]] [[Category:Birds of South America]] [[Category:Birds described in 1810|cerulean warbler]] [[Category:Taxa named by Alexander Wilson (ornithologist)]]
